name: Bulk Storage / Archive
description: Large, mostly sequential data sets such as archives, media libraries, crawl data, or cold analytical stores. Dominant metric: $/TB.

requirements.raidNote: RAID10 gives safer rebuild behavior; RAID5 buys more usable capacity at higher rebuild risk.

insights: Bulk storage is capacity-dominated - $/TB matters more than peak IOPS.
Large ingest or batch jobs tend to care more about sustained sequential write than tiny random writes.
Consumer TLC can be acceptable when write intensity is moderate and spares are planned.
NVMe speeds can be wasted if the bottleneck is network, CPU, or the storage controller.
RAID5 gives ~30% more usable capacity but RAID10 rebuilds are much safer

antiPatterns.message: U.2 NVMe may be overkill for bulk storage if the use case is network- or controller-limited.
E3.S can carry a form-factor premium; it may not be cost-justified for capacity-first archive tiers.
QLC can look attractive on $/TB, but sustained writes can fall sharply after cache exhaustion.
Industrial-grade SSDs add cost that usually does not pay back for capacity-first archive tiers.

name: Search / Web Serving
description: User-facing search, web serving, and read-heavy API use cases. Low-latency random reads are critical.
insights: This is where NVMe price parity changes everything - if 4TB SATA and 4TB NVMe are close in price, new serving boxes should be NVMe-first.
One NVMe-heavy server can replace multiple SATA boxes when random read IOPS is the limiter.
p99 latency matters more than headline throughput for user-facing requests.
Capacity needs are usually modest - don't overbuild storage that the serving tier will not use.

antiPatterns.message: SATA drives have much weaker random-read latency and IOPS than NVMe. For serving use cases, this can be a category mismatch.
Search / Web Serving is usually performance-first, not capacity-first. Very large builds can waste money on unused capacity.

name: Database / Low-Latency App
description: Databases, application state, caches, and mixed read/write services. Dominant metric: balanced latency, IOPS, and endurance.
insights: Mixed read/write services need balanced performance, not just raw capacity.
Write amplification from databases, compaction, or snapshots makes endurance more important than it looks.
NVMe helps tail latency and random I/O, especially when the app is user-facing.

antiPatterns.message: QLC can be risky for mixed database use cases because sustained writes and endurance are weaker than TLC.
All-SATA can pass aggregate capacity checks while still having worse tail latency than an NVMe-based app tier.

name: AI Training Scratch
description: High-throughput scratch space for model checkpoints, datasets, feature caches, and training pipelines. Dominant metrics: sustained write, NVMe bandwidth, and endurance.

requirements.raidNote: AI scratch is often temporary or replicated elsewhere; JBOD/RAID0 can be acceptable only when data loss is tolerable.

insights: AI scratch is not long-term storage - it is a fast working area for datasets, checkpoints, shuffles, and cache files.
Long training or preprocessing jobs can write tens of TB/day, so sustained write and TBW burn-down matter.
NVMe and high network bandwidth matter more here than for archive storage.
If the data is not replicated elsewhere, a no-redundancy layout is risky despite the speed advantage.

antiPatterns.message: QLC is a poor fit for heavy AI scratch writes because post-cache sustained write can collapse.
SATA is usually too slow for high-throughput AI scratch. NVMe is the more realistic baseline.
